figures.caption: Figure A11. LP vs activation supernode pruning. Pruning performance is similar across models for LP-defined and activation-defined protected sets. The 50% Llama-3.1-8B values use the 128-sequence calibration protocol from Table A6, so they are not directly comparable to the 64-sequence main Table 1 setting. The highest-sparsity point is a stress test outside the main operating regime.

Figure 3. Sparsity–perplexity tradeoff across models. (A) Llama-3.1-8B, (B) Mistral-7B, (C) Llama-2-7B, (D) Qwen2-7B. Each panel shows perplexity vs. structured FFN channel sparsity for SCAR-LP (solid red) and channel-adapted baselines (dashed). SCAR generally maintains lower perplexity across the models shown, with gaps often widening at high sparsity.
Figure A12. 70B scale validation. (A) Sparsity–perplexity curves. (B) Baseline perplexity relative to SCAR-LP at the same sparsity.

Figure A13. OLMo-2-7B structured pruning curves. Perplexity on WikiText-2 as a function of FFN channel sparsity for SCAR variants and baselines. SCAR-LP and Wanda-channel track each other closely across sparsities, with a small separation only at 70%; magnitude and random pruning are far worse throughout.

Figure 5. Supernodes emerge during OLMo-2-7B pretraining. Ten checkpoints of allenai/OLMo-2-1124-7B (SwiGLU, 32 FFN layers) evaluated with the same 64×512 WikiText-2 calibration used for the main 7–8B claims. (A) Median top-1% LP mass fraction rises overall with training tokens (with some non-monotonic variation mid-training); the shaded band shows the interquartile range over layers. (B) Median LP max/mean ratio (layer-level extreme-outlier diagnostic) climbs overall with training, also non-monotonically. (C) Per-layer Jaccard overlap between the top-1% at each checkpoint and the top-1% at the final (main) checkpoint. The final checkpoint has 100% self-overlap by construction; the earlier points show identities stabilizing late in training.

Figure 2. Halo redundancy and LP-vs-activation comparisons. (A) Per-layer directed redundancy from write-halo channels to the supernode core, compared with matched non-halo channels. (B) Aggregate redundancy summary: write-halo channels show higher redundancy to the protected core. (C) Conditional ablation control: halo and matched non-halo ablations preserve the supernode core, while the supernode-ablation bar shows the much larger effect of removing the core. (D) Jaccard overlap between top-1% LP channels and top-1% activation channels. (E) Spearman correlation between LP and activation scores. LP and activation scores are related but not interchangeable.

Figure 4. Pruning validation: accuracy and supernode hit-rate. (A) Average downstream accuracy across 7 benchmarks vs. sparsity on Llama-3.1-8B. (B) Perplexity at 50% sparsity with supernode hit-rate annotations; pruning more supernodes leads to much worse perplexity.
Table A11. Runtime breakdown for Llama-3.1-8B on a single A100. The forward-only row is a baseline comparison and is not included in the SCAR-specific subtotal.
